---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Duc Thai Tran"
---

```{=html}
<section class="block" style="border-bottom:none; padding-bottom:1rem;">
  <p class="eyebrow">Research</p>
  <p class="block__lead">Thai researches creative advertising, narrative advertising, strategy and effectiveness.</p>
  <p style="max-width:64ch;">He received his PhD from Macquarie University and has taught at the Macquarie Business School. While the majority of Thai's research has been in advertising, he has broad interests in other areas, such as entertainment marketing, social media marketing, and sports marketing.</p>
</section>

<section style="padding-bottom:2rem;">
  <div class="papers">

    <details class="paper">
      <summary class="paper__head">
        <span class="paper__num">01</span>
        <span class="paper__main">
          <span class="paper__title">Brand integration in narrative advertising</span>
          <span class="paper__meta"><span class="venue">American Academy of Advertising (AAA)</span> · Conference paper, 2026 · with S. Koslow &amp; A. Mirzaei</span>
        </span>
        <span class="paper__tag is-live">Accepted</span>
        <span class="paper__toggle" aria-hidden="true"></span>
      </summary>
      <div class="paper__abstract">
        <span class="label">Abstract</span>
        <p>Professional advertising creatives often use a narrative advertising format. Their goal is to present a brand in the context of an engaging story. Although there are many benefits of taking a narrative route to develop ads, a problem encountered is that the brand may be neither prominent in the ad nor congruent with the story. Thus, one may watch a narrative ad, find it engaging, but then afterward wonder what the ad was for. That is, a narrative format may be used to heighten the originality of the ad, but if one can't even identify the brand, the strategy underlying the ad was traded off. We explore the role of brands in narrative advertising using in-depth interviews with 16 creative directors. We find evidence professional creatives are aware of trade-offs between originality and strategy in the development of narrative ads. Creatives also actively manage these trade-offs when they apply a narrative advertising format.</p>
      </div>
    </details>

    <details class="paper">
      <summary class="paper__head">
        <span class="paper__num">02</span>
        <span class="paper__main">
          <span class="paper__title">How narrative ads got viral on TikTok?</span>
          <span class="paper__meta"><span class="venue">Journal of Advertising Research</span> · with S. Koslow</span>
        </span>
        <span class="paper__tag is-live">R&amp;R</span>
        <span class="paper__toggle" aria-hidden="true"></span>
      </summary>
      <div class="paper__abstract">
        <span class="label">Abstract</span>
        <p>Abstract to be added.</p>
      </div>
    </details>

    <details class="paper">
      <summary class="paper__head">
        <span class="paper__num">03</span>
        <span class="paper__main">
          <span class="paper__title">The love-hate triangle in the advertising industry</span>
        </span>
        <span class="paper__tag"></span>
        <span class="paper__toggle" aria-hidden="true"></span>
      </summary>
      <div class="paper__abstract">
        <span class="label">Abstract</span>
        <p>Abstract to be added.</p>
      </div>
    </details>

  </div>
</section>

<footer class="site-foot">
  <span>Duc Thai Tran — Sydney</span>
  <span><a href="mailto:ducthai.tran@outlook.com">ducthai.tran@outlook.com</a></span>
  <span>© 2026</span>
</footer>
```
